questions.text: What does 'Utmost Good Faith' mean in an insurance contract?
options: Only the agent should tell the truth
Both the Insurer and the Insured must disclose all material facts
Only the customer should hide their illness
The insurance company should focus on earning profit
questions.answer: Both the Insurer and the Insured must disclose all material facts
questions.explanation: Both parties are required to disclose all facts with complete honesty.

questions.text: When is 'Insurable Interest' required for taking Health Insurance?
options: Only at the time of claim
At the time of taking the policy
At the time of policy renewal
questions.answer: At the time of taking the policy
questions.explanation: Insurable interest must exist at the commencement of the policy.

questions.text: Health insurance works on which principle that compensates for the actual loss?
questions.explanation: Indemnity means financial compensation for the actual loss incurred, not for making a profit.

questions.text: Within how many days can a policyholder cancel a policy if they are dissatisfied with the terms (Free Look Period)?
questions.explanation: A policy can be cancelled within 15 days of receiving the policy documents.

questions.text: How much extra time (Grace Period) is given to pay the renewal premium for a health policy?
questions.explanation: A grace period of 30 days is provided for renewal to maintain continuity benefits.

questions.text: What is the primary function of a TPA (Third Party Administrator)?
options: Providing loans to the insurance company
questions.explanation: TPAs handle claims between the insurer and the hospital.

questions.text: What is the maximum limit for filing a complaint with the Insurance Ombudsman?
questions.explanation: The Ombudsman can hear disputes involving amounts up to 30 lakh rupees.

questions.text: What are the fees for filing a complaint with the Insurance Ombudsman?
questions.explanation: The Ombudsman process is free of cost for the convenience of the customer.

questions.text: What is the standard maximum waiting period for Pre-Existing Diseases (PED)?
questions.explanation: According to standard IRDAI guidelines, the maximum waiting period for PED is 48 months (4 years).

questions.text: What is it called when a patient receives treatment at home because they cannot be moved to a hospital?
questions.explanation: Treatment taken at home under specific conditions is called Domiciliary Hospitalization.

questions.text: How much time is required for hospitalization in a Day Care Procedure?
questions.explanation: Due to advanced technology, certain surgeries take less than 24 hours, classified as Day Care.

questions.text: What does Co-payment (Co-pay) mean?
options: Insurer will pay double the claim
The policyholder must pay a fixed percentage of the claim amount themselves
Two people pay the premium together
Getting a bonus for not making a claim
questions.answer: The policyholder must pay a fixed percentage of the claim amount themselves
questions.explanation: In Co-pay, the customer pays a specified percentage of every claim.

questions.text: What is a policy called where the entire family shares a single Sum Insured?
questions.explanation: In a family floater, there is a total limit that can be used by any family member.

questions.text: What kind of benefit does a Critical Illness Policy provide?
options: A lump sum amount upon diagnosis of the illness
questions.answer: A lump sum amount upon diagnosis of the illness
questions.explanation: The full sum insured is paid as a lump sum upon the diagnosis of critical illnesses like Cancer or Heart Attack.

questions.text: What is the full form of 'PTD' in a Personal Accident (PA) policy?
questions.explanation: It refers to a condition where a person becomes 100% disabled for life (e.g., losing both eyes).

questions.text: Who usually purchases Group Health Insurance?
questions.explanation: A group policy is a master policy taken by an employer for their staff.

questions.text: How many days before renewal must a Portability request be submitted?
questions.explanation: A notice of at least 45 days is required to switch insurance companies (Portability).

questions.text: What reward does the insurer provide next year if the policyholder does not make a claim?
questions.explanation: Renewing a policy without a claim increases the Sum Insured, known as a Cumulative Bonus.

questions.text: Under which section of the Income Tax Act is health insurance premium eligible for tax deduction?
questions.explanation: Tax benefits for health insurance come under Section 80D.

questions.text: What happens if the hospital room rent limit is exceeded during an insurance claim?
options: The entire claim will be rejected
Proportionate Deduction will apply, reducing other bill components proportionately
The hospital must reduce the bill
The insurer will pay the balance next year
questions.answer: Proportionate Deduction will apply, reducing other bill components proportionately
questions.explanation: Exceeding the room rent limit triggers a proportionate deduction, where other charges are reduced in the same ratio.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of a Moral Hazard?
options: Giving false information for a policy or making a fraudulent claim
questions.answer: Giving false information for a policy or making a fraudulent claim
questions.explanation: Moral hazard relates to the character/intent of the person, such as fraud or lying.

questions.text: What is the purpose of 'Underwriting' in Health Insurance?
options: To assess the risk and decide whether to issue the policy
Building a friendship with the customer
questions.answer: To assess the risk and decide whether to issue the policy
questions.explanation: An underwriter evaluates the risk to determine the premium and policy terms.

questions.text: Who issues the license for an insurance agent?
questions.explanation: IRDAI (Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India) issues all licenses and creates regulations.

questions.text: What is typically 'Excluded' (not covered) in a health insurance policy?
questions.explanation: Cosmetic surgeries, suicide attempts, and accidents due to alcohol are common exclusions.

questions.text: What must a patient show at the hospital for a cashless claim?
options: Health ID Card / E-card issued by the TPA
questions.answer: Health ID Card / E-card issued by the TPA
questions.explanation: Cashless facilities are availed at network hospitals by showing the Health ID card.

questions.text: What right does the principle of Subrogation give to the insurance company?
options: Right to recover money from the third party (who caused the loss)
Right to pay half the claim
questions.answer: Right to recover money from the third party (who caused the loss)
questions.explanation: After paying the claim, the insurer can recover the amount from the guilty third party.

questions.text: If a 25-year-old person takes a policy, in which category is their risk placed?
questions.explanation: Healthy and young individuals are considered Standard Risk.

questions.text: Within what time should claim intimation (information) be given to the insurer after hospitalization?
questions.explanation: It is necessary to inform the insurance company/TPA within 24 hours of hospitalization.

questions.text: Senior Citizen Health Insurance policies are usually for people of what age?
questions.explanation: People aged 60 and above are considered senior citizens.

questions.text: Who is required to fill out the proposal form?
questions.explanation: The proposer must provide all their details and fill the proposal form.

questions.text: What does 'Rider' (Add-on) mean?
options: Extra cover added to the base policy by paying extra premium
The envelope of the policy document
questions.answer: Extra cover added to the base policy by paying extra premium
questions.explanation: A rider is an additional benefit added to the main policy for an extra cost (e.g., Maternity rider).

questions.text: What is the full form of OPD?
questions.explanation: It is when a patient is not admitted but leaves after consulting the doctor.

questions.text: If a health policy has both 'Deductible' and 'Copay', which is applied first during claim calculation?
options: Deductible first, then Copay on the remaining amount
questions.answer: Deductible first, then Copay on the remaining amount
questions.explanation: First, the fixed deductible is subtracted; then, the co-pay percentage is applied to the remaining bill.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT included in AYUSH treatment?
questions.explanation: AYUSH stands for Ayurveda, Yoga/Naturopathy, Unani, Siddha, and Homeopathy. Allopathy is not part of it.

questions.text: When must a Material Fact be disclosed?
options: Only at the time of claim
While filling the proposal form before taking the policy
When the insurer sends a notice
questions.answer: While filling the proposal form before taking the policy
questions.explanation: According to the principle of utmost good faith, everything must be disclosed at the beginning.

questions.text: What is the time limit for the insurer to settle or reject a claim after receiving all necessary documents?
questions.explanation: The insurer must settle or reject the claim within 30 days of the last document submission.

questions.text: What can be the maximum sum insured in a Micro Insurance health policy?
questions.explanation: Micro-insurance, designed for the poorer sections, usually has a limit between Rs. 50,000 and 2,00,000.

questions.text: Under the Grievance Redressal Mechanism, within how many days must an insurer provide a resolution after receiving a complaint?
questions.explanation: It is mandatory to resolve a customer's complaint within 15 days of receiving it.

questions.text: On what basis is an Insurance Agent's commission calculated?
questions.explanation: Agents receive a commission based on a percentage of the premium paid by the customer.

questions.text: Which document is essential for KYC (Know Your Customer)?
questions.explanation: PAN/Aadhaar are required for identity and address verification as per AML guidelines.

questions.text: What are subsequent changes made to the policy terms and conditions called?
questions.explanation: Modifications in a policy (like name or address change) are called Endorsements.

questions.text: What is the typical waiting period for Maternity Benefit in health insurance?
questions.explanation: Most policies have a waiting period ranging from 9 months to 3-4 years for maternity coverage.

questions.text: What is an example of a Physical Hazard?
questions.explanation: Physical hazard relates to physical conditions or habits that increase the risk of a claim.

questions.text: What does it mean if a cashless claim is rejected?
options: Cashless is denied, but a reimbursement claim can be filed after discharge
The patient could go to jail
questions.answer: Cashless is denied, but a reimbursement claim can be filed after discharge
questions.explanation: Rejection of cashless doesn't mean rejection of the claim; it often means the customer pays first and then claims a refund.

questions.text: Which form does the hospital fill for cashless approval?
questions.explanation: The hospital's TPA desk sends a Pre-Authorization form to the insurer for approval.

questions.text: In which year was the first Health Insurance Policy (Mediclaim) launched in India?
questions.explanation: The Mediclaim policy was first launched in 1986.

questions.text: What is the validity period of an Agent's license?
questions.explanation: An IRDAI license is valid for 3 years, after which it must be renewed.

questions.text: What right does an insurer have if a policyholder intentionally commits fraud?
options: Pay the claim and give a warning
Declare the policy void and forfeit the premium
It is mandatory to call the police
questions.answer: Declare the policy void and forfeit the premium
questions.explanation: If fraud is proven, the contract is cancelled and the premium paid is not refunded.

questions.text: What is a Network Hospital?
options: One that has a tie-up with the insurer/TPA for cashless facilities
questions.answer: One that has a tie-up with the insurer/TPA for cashless facilities
questions.explanation: Network hospitals are those where the TPA has a tie-up to provide cashless services.

questions.text: Which entity regulates health insurance companies in India?
questions.explanation: IRDAI is the regulatory body for all insurance companies in India.
